metadata.title: Assessment of Abuse Potential of Drugs

rules.rule_text: If a drug substance is CNS-active, the new drug product containing that drug substance will likely need to undergo a thorough assessment of its abuse potential and may be subject to control under the Controlled Substances Act.
rules.source_section: Section I. Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: if a drug substance is CNS-active, the new drug product containing that drug substance will likely need to undergo a thorough assessment of its abuse potential and may be subject to control under the Controlled Substances Act (CSA) (see generally 21 U.S.C. 811).

rules.rule_text: All abuse-related study protocols and resultant data should be compiled and cross-linked by the applicant to constitute the abuse potential assessment within an NDA submission, and the assessment must include a proposal for scheduling under the CSA.
rules.source_section: Section II. Background
rules.verbatim_quote: All abuse-related study protocols and resultant data should be compiled and cross-linked by the applicant to constitute the abuse potential assessment within an NDA submission. The assessment should include a proposal for scheduling under the CSA. (Under 21 CFR 314.50(d)(5)(vii), if the drug has a potential for abuse, a description and analysis of studies or information related to abuse of the drug, including a proposal for scheduling under the CSA, are required.)
rules.exceptions: If an applicant concludes that its new drug product does not warrant scheduling under the CSA, the scheduling proposal in the NDA should state this position, and should provide justification for this position based on relevant data.

rules.rule_text: An evaluation of abuse potential is most relevant for NMEs with CNS activity that have not previously been assessed by FDA for abuse potential; if a drug substance with CNS activity is already controlled under the CSA and a different dosage strength, form, route of administration, patient population, or therapeutic indication is proposed, a modified abuse potential assessment may be necessary.
rules.source_section: Section III.A. Key Decision Points and Recommended Studies in Assessing Abuse Potential
rules.verbatim_quote: An evaluation of abuse potential is most relevant for NMEs with CNS activity that have not previously been assessed by FDA for abuse potential. However, if a drug substance with CNS activity is already controlled under the CSA and a different dosage strength, dosage form, route of administration, patient population, or therapeutic indication is proposed under an NDA or NDA supplement, a modified abuse potential assessment may be necessary and should be discussed with CSS (see Section III. B).
rules.exceptions: Generic drug products seeking approval under section 505(j) of the Federal Food, Drug & Cosmetic Act are not typically reassessed for abuse potential.

rules.rule_text: For NMEs determined to be CNS-active, the next step for sponsors should be to design and conduct abuse-related animal behavioral studies, including drug discrimination and self-administration studies.
rules.source_section: Section III.A. Question 2
rules.verbatim_quote: For new drugs determined to be CNS-active, the next step for sponsors should be to design and conduct abuse-related animal behavioral studies, such as drug discrimination and self-administration studies.

rules.rule_text: A Human Abuse Potential (HAP) study will likely be necessary to conduct if evaluation of animal and human study data identifies abuse-related signals, including: responses in animals similar to known drugs of abuse, generalization in animal discrimination studies, rewarding properties supporting self-administration, or a profile of abuse-related adverse events in clinical studies.
rules.source_section: Section III.A. Question 3
rules.verbatim_quote: If evaluation of the data from these animal and human studies identifies abuse-related signals, it is likely a HAP study will be necessary to conduct. Detailed descriptions and considerations in designing a HAP study are discussed in Section V.C.
rules.exceptions: If an evaluation of data from animal and human studies does not show evidence of abuse-related signals, a HAP study is not likely to be recommended; however, the sponsor should discuss available data with the Agency to confirm.

rules.rule_text: The abuse potential assessment submitted in the NDA must include full protocols and primary data, a statistical analysis of the data, a discussion of whether data show the drug has abuse potential, proposed labeling for the DRUG ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE section, and a proposal for scheduling under the CSA.
rules.source_section: Section III.A. Question 4
rules.verbatim_quote: The abuse potential assessment submitted by the applicant in the NDA should include full protocols and primary data, as well as a statistical analysis of the data. The assessment should also include a discussion regarding whether the data show that the drug has abuse potential, proposed labeling for the DRUG ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE section and other relevant sections, and a proposal for scheduling under the CSA, if appropriate (see Section III.E).

rules.rule_text: Sponsors are advised to discuss their planned abuse-related studies with CSS, or confirm that such studies are not warranted, at the end of phase 2 to ensure the drug is adequately assessed for abuse potential.
rules.source_section: Section III.B. When Abuse-Related Studies Should Be Conducted
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors are advised to discuss their planned abuse-related studies with CSS, or confirm that such studies are not warranted, at the end of phase 2 to ensure the drug is adequately assessed for abuse potential.

rules.rule_text: Specific abuse-related studies should be conducted only after the therapeutic dose range is determined, which is not usually known until phase 2 clinical studies are completed, at the earliest; the order of abuse-related investigations generally starts with in vitro studies, followed by animal studies, and then finally a HAP study.
rules.source_section: Section III.B. When Abuse-Related Studies Should Be Conducted
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, it is recommended that the specific abuse-related studies be conducted only after the therapeutic dose range is determined. This is not usually known until phase 2 clinical studies are completed, at the earliest. Knowledge of the therapeutic dose range should be used in determining the appropriate doses in the specific abuse-related study. The order of the abuse-related investigations generally starts with in vitro studies, followed by animal studies, and then finally by a human abuse potential study, if appropriate.

rules.rule_text: The NDA abuse potential section must include complete study protocols and all primary abuse-related data from: chemistry studies; receptor-ligand binding studies; pharmacokinetic studies in animals and humans; abuse-related studies in animals (general behavioral, drug discrimination, self-administration, physical dependence); abuse-related studies in humans (HAP, physical dependence); abuse-related AEs from clinical studies; overdose information; and assessment of the incidence of abuse during clinical studies.
rules.source_section: Section III.C. Preparing the NDA Submission
rules.verbatim_quote: The content of an abuse potential section of an NDA typically includes (or cross-references) complete study protocols and all primary abuse-related data from the following: 1. Chemistry studies 2. Receptor-ligand binding studies and functional (second messenger) studies 3. Pharmacokinetic studies in animals and humans 4. Abuse–related studies in animals... 5. Abuse-related studies in humans... 6. Abuse-related AEs from clinical studies 7. Information related to overdose, both intentional and accidental, during clinical studies 8. Assessment of the incidence of abuse during clinical studies.

rules.rule_text: Abuse-related studies and data must be submitted in the eCTD: the scheduling proposal and rationale must be in Module 1 tab 1.14.1; the summary of all abuse-related data must be in Module 2 under tab 2.7.4; and complete study protocols and data must be placed in Modules 3, 4, and 5 as appropriate.
rules.source_section: Section III.C. Preparing the NDA Submission
rules.verbatim_quote: Abuse-related studies and data should be submitted in the electronic common technical document (eCTD) as follows: The proposal and rationale related to drug scheduling should be placed in Module 1, tab 1.14.1. A summary of all abuse-related animal and human data, discussion of these data, and conclusions about the drug's abuse potential should be placed in Module 2. This should occur as a subsection under tab 2.7.4 Summary of Clinical Safety... The complete study protocols and primary data from items 1-8 above should be placed in the appropriate sections of the eCTD as follows: Module 3, chemistry; Module 4, in vitro and animal pharmacology...; Modules 5, clinical studies...

rules.rule_text: Drug abuse-related information in labeling must be described in the DRUG ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE section, which must include three subsections per 21 CFR 201.57(c)(10): (i) 9.1 Controlled Substance (stating the DEA schedule), (ii) 9.2 Abuse (types of abuse and adverse reactions, particularly susceptible populations, based primarily on human data), and (iii) 9.3 Dependence (characteristic effects from psychological and physical dependence, tolerance, and withdrawal).
rules.source_section: Section III.D. NDA Review and Product Labeling Related to Abuse Potential
rules.verbatim_quote: Abuse-related information in the labeling is primarily described in the DRUG ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE section which is comprised of three subsections, as described in 21 CFR 201.57(c)(10): "(i) 9.1 Controlled substance. If the drug is controlled by the Drug Enforcement Administration, the schedule in which it is controlled must be stated. (ii) 9.2 Abuse. This subsection must state the types of abuse that can occur with the drug and the adverse reactions pertinent to them, and must identify particularly susceptible patient populations. This subsection must be based primarily on human data and human experience, but pertinent animal data may also be used. (iii) 9.3 Dependence. This subsection must describe characteristic effects resulting from both psychological and physical dependence that occur with the drug and must identify the quantity of the drug over a period of time that may lead to tolerance or dependence, or both."

rules.rule_text: Once DEA has issued a final scheduling decision, the controlled substance symbol (C-II, C-III, C-IV, or C-V) must be included in the product labeling as required under 21 CFR 201.57(c)(10)(i) and 1302.03, and the applicant must submit a supplement to the NDA to update the labeling.
rules.source_section: Section III.E. The Drug Scheduling Process
rules.verbatim_quote: Once DEA has issued a final scheduling decision and assigned a controlled substance schedule, the controlled substance symbol, e.g., C-II, C-III, C-IV or C-V, must be included in the product labeling as required under 21 CFR 201.57(c)(10)(i) and 1302.03. In order to update the labeling following the scheduling action, a supplement to the NDA must be submitted by the applicant to update product labeling to reflect the DEA scheduling action described in the final rule or interim final rule. See 21 CFR 314.70.

rules.rule_text: The chemistry part of the abuse potential assessment section for an NME should include the chemical structure, molecular formula and weight, CAS Registry Number, chemical name, description of isomers, physicochemical properties, and description of the manufacturing process specifically addressing whether it involves a precursor that is a controlled substance or a List I or List II chemical.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A. Chemistry and Manufacturing
rules.verbatim_quote: The chemistry part of the abuse potential assessment section for an NME should include the following information about the drug substance: Chemical structure. Molecular formula and molecular weight (salt and base, as applicable). Chemical Abstracts Service (CAS) Registry Number(s). Chemical name... Description of isomers, e.g., optical, existence of diastereomers, geometric, and positional isomers. Physicochemical properties of the drug substance... Description of the manufacturing process for the drug substance, specifically addressing whether it involves a precursor that is a controlled substance or a List I or List II chemical (21 CFR 1310.02).

rules.rule_text: Comprehensive screening using in vitro receptor-ligand binding studies should be conducted to determine the pharmacological site(s) of action of the test drug, as well as its major human metabolites, in the brain; the concentration of the ligand should be at least 10 µM or equivalent to many-fold greater than the anticipated therapeutic exposure.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B. Screening with Receptor-Ligand Binding Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Comprehensive screening using in vitro receptor-ligand binding studies should be conducted to determine the pharmacological site(s) of action of the test drug, as well as its major human metabolites, in the brain... The concentration of the ligand should be at least 10 µM (or should be equivalent to many-fold greater than the anticipated therapeutic exposure).

rules.rule_text: All PK parameters utilized in designing animal abuse-related studies must be based on actual measurements, not estimations; when the NDA is submitted, all nonclinical PK studies and resultant data that informed the design and interpretation of animal abuse-related studies should be cross-linked.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C. Use of Nonclinical Pharmacokinetic Data in Animal Abuse-Related Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Protocols for animal abuse-related studies should include a justification of design elements based on nonclinical PK data, when appropriate. All PK parameters utilized should be based on actual measurements, not estimations. When the NDA is submitted, all nonclinical PK studies and resultant data that informed the design and interpretation of animal abuse-related studies should be cross-linked.

rules.rule_text: Abuse-related animal behavioral studies should be conducted when it has been determined that a test drug or any of its major metabolites are CNS-active; such studies should not typically be conducted until the end of phase 2, when final therapeutic doses are usually selected, because doses selected for testing in animal abuse-related studies should be based on plasma levels produced in humans by the highest proposed therapeutic dose.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D. Animal Abuse-Related Behavioral Pharmacology Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Abuse-related animal behavioral studies should be conducted when it has been determined that a test drug or any of its major metabolites are CNS-active... Abuse-related animal studies are not typically conducted until the end of phase 2, when the final therapeutic doses are usually selected. This is because doses selected for testing in animal abuse-related studies should be based on the plasma levels produced in humans by the highest proposed therapeutic dose.
rules.exceptions: If these animal studies are conducted with doses that do not reflect final therapeutic and supratherapeutic exposure, they will not be valid for assessing abuse potential and may need to be repeated using appropriate doses.

rules.rule_text: Abuse-related nonclinical studies must be conducted using GLP (Good Laboratory Practice) as these are nonclinical laboratory studies conducted to support approval of a drug for use in humans per 21 CFR 58.1.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.1 Application of Good Laboratory Practice (GLP)
rules.verbatim_quote: Abuse-related nonclinical studies must be conducted using GLP, as these are nonclinical laboratory studies conducted to support approval of a drug for use in humans. See 21 CFR 58.1.

rules.rule_text: Typically, abuse-related animal studies are conducted in rats; nonhuman primates should be reserved only for those limited cases where there is clear evidence that they would be predictive of human abuse liability and the rodent model is inadequate; the study protocol should justify the species selected.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.1 General Considerations – Selection of Animals
rules.verbatim_quote: Typically, abuse-related animal studies are conducted in rats, because this species has been validated for use in these studies... Nonhuman primates should be reserved only for those limited cases where there is clear evidence that they would be predictive of human abuse liability and the rodent model is inadequate. The study protocol should justify the species selected, especially if a non-rodent species is proposed.

rules.rule_text: The doses used in animal abuse-related studies should be derived from the Cmax produced by the highest proposed therapeutic dose; abuse-related studies in animals typically test doses that produce Cmax levels equivalent to, as well as at least 2-3 times greater than, the Cmax produced by the highest proposed therapeutic dose.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.1 General Considerations – Selection of Drug Doses
rules.verbatim_quote: The doses used in animal abuse-related studies should be selected on the basis of the specific test being conducted (see below). For regulatory purposes, the doses of a test drug that will be evaluated in animal abuse-related studies should be derived from the Cmax produced by the highest proposed therapeutic dose... abuse-related studies in animals typically test doses that produce Cmax levels that are equivalent to, as well as at least 2-3 times greater than, the Cmax produced by the highest proposed therapeutic dose.
rules.exceptions: This principle does not apply for a self-administration study. For a test drug with partial agonist activity, evaluating decrements in dose may be more appropriate.

rules.rule_text: Abuse-related studies must include a positive control drug group and vehicle group for comparison with the test drug group; the positive control group must produce results that are statistically significantly different from the vehicle group to establish assay sensitivity; the positive control drug should be in the same pharmacological class as the test drug and scheduled under the CSA.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.1 General Considerations – Control Groups
rules.verbatim_quote: Abuse-related studies should include a positive control drug group and vehicle group for comparison with the test drug group. In order for a study to be considered valid, the positive control group should produce results that are statistically significantly different from the vehicle group to establish that the study has "assay sensitivity" (i.e., the ability to demonstrate that an abuse signal can be produced under study conditions, to ensure that the abuse potential of the test drug can be determined)... The drug selected as the positive control should be in the same pharmacological class as the test drug, whenever possible, and should be scheduled under the CSA.
rules.exceptions: For a test drug with a mechanism of action that is novel or does not correspond to a drug currently controlled under the CSA, the Agency will consider sponsor proposals for an alternative positive control.

rules.rule_text: Data collection in animal behavioral studies should occur at Tmax, with additional measurements made before and afterward to ensure full characterization of the test drug; data from animal PK studies should inform the time points at which observations are made.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.1 General Considerations – Timing of Data Collection
rules.verbatim_quote: Data collection in animal behavioral studies should occur at Tmax, with additional measurements made before and afterward to ensure full characterization of the test drug. Since Tmax is determined by route of administration, data from animal PK studies should inform the time points at which observations are made.

rules.rule_text: Final testing in self-administration and drug discrimination studies should be conducted using a standard FR10 (fixed ratio 10) schedule of reinforcement, rather than schedules with greater response requirements or progressive ratio.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.1 General Considerations – Schedules of Reinforcement for Operant Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: final testing should be conducted using a standard FR10, rather than schedules of reinforcement with greater response requirements (e.g., use of fixed ratio values greater than 10, or use of progressive ratio).

rules.rule_text: In the self-administration study, the intravenous route of administration should be used in order to optimize the temporal association between active lever-pressing and immediate drug effects; the doses of the test drug should be fractions of the doses that produce plasma levels similar to those produced by the human therapeutic dose.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.3 Evaluation of Rewarding Drug Effects – Self-Administration Study
rules.verbatim_quote: The intravenous route of administration should be used in order to optimize the temporal association between active lever-pressing and immediate drug effects. The doses of the test drug should be fractions of the doses that produce plasma levels similar to those produced by the human therapeutic dose. This is done to allow for the repeated administration of the test drug without the risk of immediate overdose, and to prevent immediate satiation from a single dose, which would obscure the ability to assess reinforcement.
rules.exceptions: Certain classes of drugs with hallucinogenic properties do not typically produce animal self-administration (e.g., 5HT2A agonists), or are only self-administered by animals under limited conditions.

rules.rule_text: In drug discrimination studies, full generalization to the training drug occurs when the animal lever presses >80% on the training drug lever; no generalization occurs when lever pressing is <20% on the training drug lever; partial generalization between 60-80% suggests the test drug produces an interoceptive cue with some similarity to the training drug and may have abuse potential.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.4 Evaluation of Similarity of Effects to Known Drugs of Abuse – Drug Discrimination
rules.verbatim_quote: "Full generalization" to the training drug occurs when the animal lever presses >80% (8 of 10 times) on the lever associated with training drug. "No generalization" occurs when the animal lever presses <20% on the training drug lever (e.g., >80% on the vehicle lever). "Partial generalization" occurs in between these poles. For regulatory purposes, partial generalization between 60-80% suggests that the test drug produces an interoceptive cue that has some similarity to the training drug.

rules.rule_text: In the physical dependence study, drug administration should typically occur for at least four weeks at stable doses that produce plasma levels similar to those produced by therapeutic (and possibly supratherapeutic) doses; behavioral observations should begin several days before drug discontinuation and continue daily for at least 7 days or until the test drug is eliminated.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D.5 Evaluation of Physical Dependence and Withdrawal Behaviors – Physical Dependence Study
rules.verbatim_quote: Drug administration should typically occur for at least four weeks at stable doses that produce plasma levels similar to those produced by therapeutic (and possibly supratherapeutic) doses. Withdrawal is preferentially initiated in animals through abrupt drug discontinuation... Behavioral observations should begin several days before drug discontinuation and continue daily for at least 7 days or for a duration equivalent until the test drug is eliminated.

rules.rule_text: All clinical safety and efficacy studies should be evaluated for CNS-related adverse events that may suggest the test drug produces effects that will be sought out for abuse purposes; abuse-related AE data from clinical safety and efficacy studies should be systematically categorized, tabulated, and analyzed to determine if AE patterns exist.
rules.source_section: Section V.B. Abuse-Related Adverse Events in Clinical Safety and Efficacy Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: All clinical safety and efficacy studies should be evaluated for CNS-related AEs that may suggest the test drug produces effects that will be sought out for abuse purposes... Abuse-related AE data from clinical safety and efficacy studies should be systematically categorized, tabulated, and analyzed to determine if AE patterns exist within different human populations following administration of the test drug.

rules.rule_text: HAP studies should not be conducted prior to the end of phase 2 clinical studies; they should ensure the doses used are the final proposed therapeutic doses and that safety has been established following phase 1 studies in healthy individuals and phase 2 studies in individuals with the disease of interest.
rules.source_section: Section V.C. Human Abuse Potential Study in Recreational Drug Users
rules.verbatim_quote: During drug development, it is generally advised that HAP studies not be conducted prior to the end of phase 2 clinical studies. This should ensure that the doses used in the HAP study are based on the final proposed therapeutic doses and have established safety following phase 1 studies in healthy individuals and phase 2 studies in individuals with the disease of interest.

rules.rule_text: HAP studies should be conducted in experienced recreational drug users who have a recent history of using drugs in the same general pharmacological class as the test drug; it is not recommended that drug-naive subjects be used in HAP studies because this population has not been validated scientifically as being able to provide accurate information on the abuse potential of a drug.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2 Subjects and Sample Size
rules.verbatim_quote: HAP studies should be conducted in experienced recreational drug users who have a recent history of using drugs in the same general pharmacological class as the test drug... It is not recommended that drug-naïve subjects be used in HAP studies because this population has not been validated scientifically as being able to provide accurate information on the abuse potential of a drug.

rules.rule_text: HAP subjects should be excluded from participation if they are currently dependent on a drug (other than caffeine or nicotine) or if they are in drug treatment or recovery; additional exclusionary criteria must include positive drug urine screen, positive breath alcohol test, signs of drug withdrawal, serious psychiatric or neurological conditions, and any medical condition that could be exacerbated by drug exposure.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2 Subjects and Sample Size
rules.verbatim_quote: Individuals should be excluded from participation in the study if they are currently dependent on a drug (other than caffeine or nicotine) or if they are in drug treatment or recovery. Additional exclusionary criteria specific to a HAP study should include positive drug urine screen, positive breath alcohol test, signs of drug withdrawal, serious psychiatric or neurological conditions, and any medical condition that could be exacerbated by drug exposure or interfere with study procedures.

rules.rule_text: In the Qualification Phase of a HAP study, each subject should be primarily evaluated using a bipolar Drug Liking VAS of 0-100, such that placebo should produce a score between 40-60 points, the positive control should produce a score outside of the placebo range, and there should be a difference of at least 15 points between placebo and positive control response; only subjects who produce these results should participate in the Treatment Phase.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.3 Study Design – Qualification Phase
rules.verbatim_quote: Each subject should be primarily evaluated using a bipolar Drug Liking VAS of 0-100, such that placebo should produce a score between 40-60 points, the positive control should produce a score outside of the placebo range, and there should be a difference of at least 15 points between placebo and positive control response. Only those subjects who produce these results should participate in the Treatment Phase.

rules.rule_text: The Treatment Phase must be designed as a randomized, double-blind, double-dummy, placebo- and active-controlled, crossover study; study treatments must include placebo, one or two doses of the positive control, and at least three doses of the test drug.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.3 Study Design – Treatment Phase
rules.verbatim_quote: The Treatment Phase is usually designed as a randomized, double-blind, double-dummy, placebo- and active-controlled, crossover study. The study treatments should include placebo, one or two doses of the positive control and at least three doses of the test drug.

rules.rule_text: For regulatory purposes, the doses of a test drug evaluated in human abuse-related studies should be based on the highest proposed therapeutic dose in humans, as well as a dose 2-3 times that dose if this can be done safely; a standard washout period between sessions should be calculated on the basis of the drug with the longest half-life, typically within 5 half-lives.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.3 Study Design – Treatment Phase
rules.verbatim_quote: For regulatory purposes, the doses of a test drug that will be evaluated in human abuse-related studies should be based on the highest proposed therapeutic dose in humans, as well as a dose 2-3 times that dose, if this can be done safely... A standard washout period between sessions should be calculated on the basis of the drug with the longest half-life, so that adequate drug elimination between study sessions will be achieved (typically within 5 half-lives).
rules.exceptions: Lower doses may be appropriate when the drug has a narrow therapeutic index or produces differences in the PK profile between subject subgroups.

rules.rule_text: The VAS for 'Drug Liking' should be selected as the primary measure in HAP studies; the statistical analysis of a HAP study should address whether the positive control produces reliable abuse-related responses compared to placebo, whether the test drug produces abuse-related responses smaller than the positive control, and whether the test drug produces responses similar to placebo.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.4 Outcome Measurements and Section V.C.5 Analysis of Data
rules.verbatim_quote: The VAS for "Drug Liking" should be selected as the primary measure... The statistical analysis of a HAP study should address whether: The known drug of abuse (positive control) produces reliable abuse-related responses compared to placebo. The test drug produces abuse-related responses that are smaller than the positive control. The test drug produces abuse-related responses that are similar to placebo.

rules.rule_text: The statistical model used in HAP studies must be a linear mixed-effects model including period, sequence, and treatment as fixed effects, and subject as a random effect; the primary analyses must be based on testing differences between the means from the primary measure(s) at the peak of drug response effects (Emax) at a significance level of 0.05 (1-sided).
rules.source_section: Section V.C.5 Analysis of Data – Primary Analyses
rules.verbatim_quote: The statistical model that should be used in HAP studies is a linear mixed-effects model, which includes period, sequence, and treatment as fixed effects, and subject as a random effect. The primary analyses of abuse potential should be based on testing the differences between the means from the primary measure(s) at the peak of drug response effects (Emax) produced by the test drug, the positive control, and placebo, using proper tests and appropriate statistical methods at a significance level of 0.05 (1-sided).

rules.rule_text: HAP studies should be statistically evaluated as safety studies with the null hypothesis constructed on the presumption that the test drug produces abuse potential similar to the positive control; all statistical analyses including primary, secondary, any alternative and supportive analyses should be pre-specified.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.5 Analysis of Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Statistically, HAP studies should be evaluated as safety studies. Thus, the null hypotheses for the test drug should be constructed based on the presumption that the test drug produces abuse potential similar to the positive control and therefore differentiates from placebo... All statistical analyses including primary, secondary, any alternative and supportive analyses should be pre-specified.

rules.rule_text: An abuse potential assessment must include an evaluation of whether a drug produces physical dependence, in order to: provide accurate labeling information regarding risks associated with abrupt drug discontinuation, and provide information FDA will use in determining scheduling under the CSA and which schedule should be recommended.
rules.source_section: Section V.E. Clinical Evaluation of Physical Dependence
rules.verbatim_quote: An abuse potential assessment should include an evaluation of whether a drug produces physical dependence, in order to: 1) provide accurate information in labeling regarding the risks associated with abrupt drug discontinuation (and the possible need for tapered discontinuation), and 2) provide information that FDA will use in determining whether a drug will be recommended for scheduling under the CSA, and if so, which schedule should be recommended (see 21 U.S.C. 811(c)(7)).

rules.rule_text: Human physical dependence evaluation should use abrupt drug discontinuation rather than tapered discontinuation or precipitated withdrawal in order to produce naturalistic conditions; the duration of observation during drug discontinuation should persist for a period equivalent to at least 5 half-lives of the test drug.
rules.source_section: Section V.E. Clinical Evaluation of Physical Dependence
rules.verbatim_quote: Use of abrupt drug discontinuation rather than tapered discontinuation, or precipitated withdrawal through antagonist administration, is generally preferable in order to produce naturalistic conditions under which patients stop taking medication. The duration of observation during drug discontinuation should persist for a period equivalent to at least 5 half-lives of the test drug when the drug has been fully eliminated.
rules.exceptions: When abrupt discontinuation may pose a safety concern due to return of disease symptoms, a dedicated physical dependence study conducted in healthy controls may be recommended.

rules.rule_text: Post-marketing data including reports of abuse, addiction, and deaths from U.S. and other sources should be placed into Module 5, postmarketing experience, of the NDA; such post-marketing data are not a substitute for a thorough abuse potential assessment conducted prior to submission of an NDA.
rules.source_section: Section VI. Post-Marketing and Illicit Drug Abuse Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Reports of abuse, addiction, and deaths from the U.S. and other sources, including those from outside the U.S., should be placed into Module 5, postmarketing experience... Although post-marketing data from US or foreign sources can provide important abuse-related information, they are not a substitute for a thorough abuse potential assessment conducted prior to submission of an NDA.
